why_holdout_still_failed_even_after_oos_.summary: The updated core stack improved OOS materially, but that improvement did not carry into the final holdout window.
evidence: Out-of-sample average_return_per_trade improved from -0.0909 to 0.1342, but final holdout remained negative at -0.4595.
Out-of-sample profit_factor improved from 0.9629 to 1.0270, but final holdout profit_factor remained weak at 0.8420.
Win rate stayed almost flat between OOS and holdout for the updated core stack (38.3984 vs 38.3721), while max_drawdown worsened from 31.2179 to 34.4658.
This pattern suggests the main weakness is not simple signal scarcity; the stack behaves materially worse in the holdout regime despite similar hit-rate.

why_dominant: The updated core stack already improved OOS without touching Layer 5 or Layer 4, which weakens the case that generic exit or sizing redesign is the first target now.
Holdout degradation appears window-specific: the same simplified entry stack that became OOS-positive still fails in the later 2024-2025 holdout.
Win rate stays broadly flat while drawdown and payoff quality worsen, which is more consistent with the stack being exposed to an unfavorable regime/context than with a pure hit-rate problem.
why_not_primary_entry_fragility: Entry simplification already improved OOS materially, so entry fragility is no longer the strongest first-order explanation for the remaining holdout gap.
why_not_primary_exit_hold_behaviour: Exit behaviour may still contribute, but touching Layer 5 now would broaden scope before isolating whether the holdout weakness is driven by regime/context mismatch.
why_not_primary_drawdown_control: Drawdown is still too high, but immediately opening Layer 4 or a portfolio-risk redesign would violate the current single-scope discipline and skip the likely regime-specific root cause.

redesign_options_compared.label: Refine Layer 1 for holdout regime sensitivity
redesign_options_compared.scope_boundary: Keep the updated core stack intact and test whether a narrower market-regime interpretation improves final holdout resilience.
redesign_options_compared.why_considered: Most aligned with the evidence that the same stack behaves differently in OOS versus holdout.

redesign_options_compared.label: Re-open Layer 5 exit/hold behaviour
redesign_options_compared.scope_boundary: Would revisit ATR trailing or time-stop behaviour.
redesign_options_compared.why_not_selected: Too early before isolating whether the later-window weakness is actually regime/context driven.

redesign_options_compared.label: Re-open Layer 4 drawdown control / sizing overlay
redesign_options_compared.why_not_selected: Broader than needed and explicitly outside the current frozen-layer discipline.

redesign_options_compared.label: Continue entry reshaping immediately
redesign_options_compared.scope_boundary: Would reopen entry redesign beyond the newly updated core stack.
redesign_options_compared.why_not_selected: The updated core stack already improved OOS, so the strongest remaining evidence points elsewhere first.

what_it_means: Do not change Layer 3, Layer 4, or Layer 5 yet.
Do not reopen broad entry redesign yet.
Open a narrow planning cycle that tests whether the updated core stack needs a stricter or more context-aware Layer 1 interpretation for the holdout window.
why_selected: It is the narrowest redesign target that still addresses the specific OOS-vs-holdout divergence now visible in the updated core stack.
It directly targets the residual problem: the stack survives OOS better now, but still fails in the later unseen market environment.
It preserves current discipline by not touching sizing or exit before the likely regime mismatch is examined.

official_next_action.note: The next cycle should plan a narrow Layer 1 holdout regime-sensitivity refinement on top of the updated provisional core stack.
